mainTopic: (continues) האח מבי חוזאיי · הפקדת פירות וחסרונות · שתיית יין ושמן מזוקק
overview: (1) A story about a man claiming to be a brother and demanding inheritance, leading to a dispute over improvements made to the property. (2) The Mishnah lists the standard deductions for loss in deposited grains, and the Gemara analyzes the reasons for these deductions. (3) The Mishnah discusses deductions for wine and oil, and the Gemara analyzes the dispute between Rabbi Yehuda and the Sages regarding mixing dregs. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: A story about a man claiming to be a brother and demanding inheritance, leading to a dispute over improvements made to the property.
sugyaBoundaries.openingFormula: מרי בר איסק אתא ליה אחא מבי חוזאיי

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Mishnah lists the standard deductions for loss in deposited grains, and the Gemara analyzes the reasons for these deductions.
The Mishnah discusses deductions for wine and oil, and the Gemara analyzes the dispute between Rabbi Yehuda and the Sages regarding mixing dregs. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: אַבָּיֵי's challenge to רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling
steps.stepSummary: אַבָּיֵי challenges רַב חִסְדָּא's application of the Mishna, arguing that the Mishna applies only when the adult sons know the minors exist and forgo payment, which is not the case here.
steps.whatsHappening: אַבָּיֵי raises a difficulty against רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling. He argues that the Mishna's rule of sharing improvements is based on the adults knowing the minors exist and choosing to forgo payment.
steps.deeperAnalysis: אַבָּיֵי identifies a crucial distinction. The Mishna assumes the adult sons are aware of the minors' existence. Their sharing of improvements is an act of familial generosity.
steps.whatToRemember: The key distinction is knowledge: did the improver know the other heir existed?
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces רַב חִסְדָּא to refine his ruling or find a different basis for it.

phrases.english: And likewise Rabba said: They enhanced the middle.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר לֵיהּ אַבָּיֵי: מִי דָּמֵי? הָתָם,
phrases.english: Abaye said to him: Are they similar? There,

phrases.aramaic: גְּדוֹלִים גַּבֵּי קְטַנִּים יָדְעִי וְקָא מָחֲלִי,
phrases.english: adults with minors know and are waiving,
here who knows that he is afraid?

steps.triggerLanguage: וְכֵן אָמַר רַבָּה: הִשְׁבִּיחוּ לָאֶמְצַע. אָמַר לֵיהּ אַבָּיֵי: מִי דָּמֵי? הָתָם, גְּדוֹלִים גַּבֵּי קְטַנִּים יָדְעִי וְקָא מָחֲלִי, הָכָא מִי יָדַע דְּלֵיחִיל?

rashi.hebrew: וכן אמר רבה השביחו לאמצע - לא גרסינן שאין זה לשון גמרא להביא דברי האמוראים סיוע למשנה ועוד רבה תלמידיה דרב חסדא הוה והיכי אמר רב חסדא שמעתא משמיה ואי גרס ליה וכן אמר רב גרסינן שהיה רבו של רב חסדא משום דאיכא למ"ד בבבא בתרא (דף קמג:) לא שנו אלא ששבחו נכסים מחמת נכסים אבל שבחו מחמת עצמן של אחים השביחו לעצמן אמרה רב חסדא להא דרב הכא למימר דלית לן ההיא שמעתא אלא אפילו השביחו מחמת טורחן של משביחין כי הכא דשתל פרדס השביחו לאמצע:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the text should read רב, not רבה, because רבה was a student of רב חסדא and would not be cited as a source for רב חסדא's ruling, and רב's opinion is needed to counter the view in בבא בתרא that improvements from one's own labor belong to the improver.

rashi.hebrew: ידעו - אלו שיטלו חלקם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that ידעו refers to the adult sons knowing that the minors would take their share of the inheritance.

rashi.hebrew: מי ידע - שיש לו אחים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that מי ידע means מרי בר איסק did not know that he had a brother.

tosafot.hebrew: וכן אמר רבה גרס - רש"י גרס וכן אמר רב ופליג אמ"ד בפ' מי שמת (ב"ב דף קמג: ושם ד"ה לא) נכסים ששבחו מחמת עצמן של אחין השבח לעצמן וקאמר דאפי' שבחו מחמת טרחם של משביחין כי הכא דשתל פרדיסא השביחו לאמצע וזהו תימה שחולק רבא על רב דהתם רבא קאמר ולא מייתי התם מילתיה דרב ונראה דגרס רבה וכן בספר ר"ת ולא מדברי רב חסדא הוא שהגמ' מביא שמעשה כיוצא בזה בא לפני רבה ופסק דהשביחו לאמצע כמו רב חסדא והביאו הגמרא משום קושית אביי דא"ל אביי לרבה מי דמי כו' ולא חשש רבה מקושיותיו ודן דהשביחו לאמצע:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות argues that the text should read רבה, not רב, because רבה was a student of רב חסדא and the Gemara cites a case that came before רבה where he ruled like רב חסדא, and רבה did not retract despite אַבָּיֵי's challenge.

meforshim.interplaySummary: רש״י and תוספות disagree on the correct text: רש״י reads רב to align with a ruling in בבא בתרא, while תוספות reads רבה to match the context of רב חסדא's student and the case cited.

steps.title: רַבִּי אַמֵּי's challenge to רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling
steps.stepSummary: רַבִּי אַמֵּי challenges רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling by citing a stronger precedent: relatives who tend to a captive's property are paid as sharecroppers, so מרי בר איסק should at least be reimbursed for his labor.
steps.whatsHappening: The case is brought to רַבִּי אַמֵּי. He challenges רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling by citing a precedent where relatives who tend to a captive's property are paid as sharecroppers.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי אַמֵּי uses a kal v'chomer. If relatives who tend to a captive's property are paid as sharecroppers, then מרי בר איסק should definitely be compensated. רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling seems too harsh.

keyTerms.meaning: they are appraised as a sharecropper [and paid accordingly]

steps.whatToRemember: The precedent is about relatives tending to a captive's property.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces רַב חִסְדָּא to justify why מרי בר איסק gets nothing for his labor.

phrases.aramaic: אִגַּלְגַּל מִלְּתָא וּמְטָא לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי אַמֵּי.
phrases.english: The matter rolled and came before Rabbi Ami.
He said to them: Greater than this they said:
They appraise for them as a sharecropper,
Now that his own we do not give to him?

steps.triggerLanguage: אִגַּלְגַּל מִלְתָּא וּמְטָא לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי אַמֵּי. אֲמַר לְהוּ: גְּדוֹלָה מִזּוֹ אָמְרוּ...

rashi.hebrew: גדולה מזו - קרוב היורד לנכסי שבוי שאם יבאו הבעלים יטלו שלהם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the precedent of relatives tending a captive's property involves a case where the owner might return and reclaim everything, making the relatives' compensation as sharecroppers a significant leniency.

rashi.hebrew: אמרו שמין להם כאריס הכא דידיה לא יהבינן ליה - בתמיה זה שעשה והשביחן לדעת עצמו לא יהבי' ליה כאריס בחלק השני:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that רַבִּי אַמֵּי's rhetorical question emphasizes that if strangers are compensated for improving property that isn't theirs, then מרי בר איסק, who improved property partly his own, should certainly be compensated as a sharecropper for his half.

tosafot.hebrew: אגלגל מלתא - פסק של רב חסדא ומטא לקמיה דרבי אמי:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות clarifies that the phrase אִגַּלְגַּל מִלְּתָא refers to the specific ruling of רַב חִסְדָּא being brought before רַבִּי אַמֵּי for review.

steps.title: רַב חִסְדָּא's defense of his ruling
steps.stepSummary: רַב חִסְדָּא defends his ruling by distinguishing the captive case: there, the relatives acted with court authorization; here, מרי בר איסק acted without authorization. Additionally, the brother was a minor, and courts don't authorize relatives to manage a minor's property.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב חִסְדָּא responds to רַבִּי אַמֵּי's challenge. He distinguishes the captive case: those relatives acted with court authorization.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב חִסְדָּא provides two distinctions. First, the captive case involved court authorization, which creates an obligation to pay. Second, the brother was a minor, and courts don't authorize relatives to manage a minor's property.

keyTerms.meaning: one does not authorize a relative to manage a minor's property

steps.whatToRemember: The key distinctions are authorization and the minor's status.
steps.whyThisMatters: This resolves the challenge from רַבִּי אַמֵּי and justifies רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling.

phrases.english: They returned it before Rav Ḥisda.
He said to them: Are they similar?

phrases.aramaic: הָתָם בִּרְשׁוּת נָחֵית, הָכָא לָאו בִּרְשׁוּת נָחֵית.
phrases.english: There, with authorization it descended; here, not with authorization it descended.

phrases.aramaic: וְעוֹד: קָטָן הוּא, וְאֵין מוֹרִידִין קָרוֹב לְנִכְסֵי קָטָן.
phrases.english: And furthermore: He is a minor, and they do not cause a relative to descend to a minor's property.

steps.triggerLanguage: אַהְדְּרוּהָ לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַב חִסְדָּא. אֲמַר לְהוּ: מִי דָּמֵי? הָתָם בִּרְשׁוּת נָחֵית, הָכָא לָאו בִּרְשׁוּת נָחֵית...

rashi.hebrew: ברשות ב"ד נחתי - דמורידין קרוב לנכסי שבוי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in the case of the captive, the relatives acted with the authorization of the בית דין, as they are permitted to manage the property of a captive.

rashi.hebrew: הכא לא ברשות נחית - כשירד זה לתוכו לא נמלך בב"ד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that in the current case, מרי בר איסק did not consult the בית דין before acting.

rashi.hebrew: ועוד - אם בא לימלך לא היו מורידין אותו דהא אחיו קטן הוה ואין מורידין קרוב לנכסי קטן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י adds that even if מרי בר איסק had consulted the בית דין, they would not have authorized him because the brother was a minor, and relatives are not permitted to manage a minor's property.

steps.title: רַבִּי אַמֵּי's concession
steps.stepSummary: רַבִּי אַמֵּי concedes that רַב חִסְדָּא is correct, noting that he was unaware the brother was a minor when he issued his challenge.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבִּי אַמֵּי accepts רַב חִסְדָּא's distinction. He notes that he wasn't told the brother was a minor when he issued his challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי אַמֵּי concedes. His challenge was based on incomplete information. Once he learns the brother was a minor, the distinction holds: no authorization was given, so מרי בר איסק gets no payment.

keyTerms.meaning: they did not complete [the details] before me

steps.whatToRemember: רַבִּי אַמֵּי's challenge was based on incomplete information.
steps.whyThisMatters: This concludes the sugya, affirming רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling.

phrases.aramaic: אַהְדְּרוּהָ לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי אַמֵּי, אֲמַר לְהוּ:
phrases.english: They returned it before Rabbi Ami, he said to them:
They did not complete it before me, for a minor he was.

steps.triggerLanguage: אַהְדְּרוּהָ לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי אַמֵּי, אֲמַר לְהוּ: לָא סַיְּימוּהָ קַמַּי דְּקָטָן הוּא.

steps.title: The Mishna's standard deductions for deposited produce
steps.stepSummary: The Mishna establishes the standard rates of deduction for loss in deposited grains, varying by type of produce, and notes that deductions depend on both the quantity and the time elapsed.
steps.whatsHappening: The Mishna introduces the topic of הפקדת פירות and lists the standard deductions for loss.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishna sets the baseline halacha: a bailee may deduct standard losses from deposited produce. The rates vary by grain type. The final clause emphasizes that deductions scale with both the volume deposited and the duration of the deposit.
steps.whatToRemember: The Mishna's rates are per kor per year.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that the Mishna's rates seem high for some grains, which the Gemara will challenge.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the baseline for the entire sugya's analysis of loss deductions.

phrases.english: MISHNA: One who deposits produce with his fellow,
this one deducts for him its deficiencies:
For wheat and for rice –
nine half-kavim per kor,
For barley and for millet –
nine kavim per kor,
For spelt and for flaxseed –
three se'ah per kor,

phrases.aramaic: הַכֹּל לְפִי הַמִּדָּה וְהַכֹּל לְפִי הַזְּמַן.
phrases.english: All according to the measure and all according to the time.

steps.triggerLanguage: מַתְנִי׳ הַמַּפְקִיד פֵּירוֹת אֵצֶל חֲבֵירוֹ, הֲרֵי זֶה יוֹצִיא לוֹ חֶסְרוֹנוֹת: לְחִטִּים וּלְאוֹרֶז – תִּשְׁעָה חֲצָאֵי קַבִּין לְכוֹר, לִשְׂעוֹרִין וּלְדוֹחַן – תִּשְׁעָה קַבִּין לְכוֹר, לְכוּסְּמִין וְלְזֶרַע פִּשְׁתָּן – שָׁלֹשׁ סְאִין לְכוֹר, הַכֹּל לְפִי הַמִּדָּה וְהַכֹּל לְפִי הַזְּמַן.

rashi.hebrew: מתני' הרי זה יוציא לו חסרונות - כשיחזירם לו יפחות כמה הם רגילים לחסור ובגמ' מוקי לה בשעירבן עם פירותיו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishna's phrase יוציא לו חסרונות means the bailee deducts the standard expected loss when returning the produce, and notes that the Gemara later clarifies this applies when the bailee mixed the deposited produce with his own.

rashi.hebrew: הכל לפי המדה - וכן לכל כור וכור:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the clause הכל לפי המדה means the deduction rate applies proportionally to every כור deposited.

rashi.hebrew: הכל לפי הזמן - שהניחן בידו לכל שנה ושנה יניח לו כך:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that הכל לפי הזמן means the deduction is calculated annually based on how many years the produce remained in the bailee's possession.

steps.title: Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri and Rabbi Yehuda's dissenting views
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri argues that deductions should be fixed per kor regardless of total volume, while Rabbi Yehuda argues that large deposits incur no deductions because the produce increases.
steps.whatsHappening: The Mishna presents two dissenting opinions on the deduction rules.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן בֶּן נוּרִי challenges the scaling rule: mice eat a fixed amount regardless of volume, so the deduction should be fixed. רַבִּי יְהוּדָה challenges the deduction itself for large volumes, arguing that the produce increases which offsets the loss.
steps.whatToRemember: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן בֶּן נוּרִי focuses on the fixed nature of mouse damage. רַבִּי יְהוּדָה focuses on the increase in large volumes.
steps.confusionAlert: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן בֶּן נוּרִי is a Tanna here, not the Amora רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן.
steps.whyThisMatters: These dissenting views set up the Gemara's later analysis of the reasons for deductions and the conditions under which they apply.

phrases.english: Rabbi Yoḥanan ben Nuri said:
And what do the mice care?

phrases.aramaic: וַהֲלֹא אוֹכְלוֹת בֵּין מֵהַרְבֵּה וּבֵין מִקִּמְעָה!
phrases.english: And don't they eat whether from much and whether from little!

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא אֵינוֹ יוֹצִיא לוֹ חֶסְרוֹנוֹת אֶלָּא
phrases.english: Rather, he does not deduct for him his decreases except
for one kor alone.
Rabbi Yehuda says:
If the measure was large,

phrases.aramaic: אֵינוֹ מוֹצִיא לוֹ חֶסְרוֹנוֹת, מִפְּנֵי שֶׁמּוֹתִירוֹת.
phrases.english: he does not deduct for him his decreases, because of that they remain.
phrases.notes: The reference to 'they remain' alludes to the increase in produce that offsets the decrease.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן בֶּן נוּרִי: וְכִי מָה אִכְפַּת לָהֶן לָעַכְבָּרִין? וַהֲלֹא אוֹכְלוֹת בֵּין מֵהַרְבֵּה וּבֵין מִקִּמְעָה! אֶלָּא אֵינוֹ יוֹצִיא לוֹ חֶסְרוֹנוֹת אֶלָּא לְכוֹר אֶחָד בִּלְבַד. רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אוֹמֵר: אִם הָיְתָה מִדָּה מְרוּבָּה, אֵינוֹ מוֹצִיא לוֹ חֶסְרוֹנוֹת, מִפְּנֵי שֶׁמּוֹתִירוֹת.

rashi.hebrew: ר' יוחנן בן נורי אמר - אהכל לפי המדה פליג:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי יוחנן בן נורי is challenging the general principle of deducting losses proportionally to the volume of produce stored.

rashi.hebrew: אם היתה מדה מרובה - שהפקיד אצלו הרבה ובגמרא מפרש י' כורים לא יוציא לו חסרונות מפני שהם מותירות שבימות הגורן כשמפקיד החיטין יבשין ובימות הגשמים כשמחזיר נופחות ובאכילת עכברים אינן נחסרים כל כך לכל כור וכור דכולי האי לא אכלי עכברים מי' כורין הלכך נפיחתו משלמת חסרון המגיע לשני כורים או שלשה שהעכברים אוכלין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי יהודה holds that for large volumes (like ten כורים), the natural expansion of the grain during rainy seasons compensates for the fixed amount eaten by mice, so no deduction is made.

tosafot.hebrew: וכי מה אכפת להו לעכברים - וטעמייהו דרבנן מפרש בירושלמי הני עכברי רשיעי נינהו כד חמיין עיבור לא מסתייהו דאכלי אלא קריין לחבריהון דאכלי עמהון והא דאמרינן בגמרא אמרו לו הרבה אובדות מהם הרבה מתפזרות מהם י"ל דלדבריו דרבי יוחנן קאמר אפילו לית לך טעמא דמפרש בירושלמי מ"מ הרבה אובדות מהם:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות cites the ירושלמי to explain the Chachamim's view that mice are 'wicked' and eat more when the volume is larger, and notes that even without this reason, the Chachamim argue that larger volumes inherently suffer greater losses from scattering.

tosafot.hebrew: אלא לכור בלבד - וא"ת אם כן בפחות מכור נמי יוציא כמו לכור וי"ל דאין להם ריוח להטמן בתוכה ומ"מ לפי חשבון הראוי להתחסר יוציא דאי אפשר שלא יתחסרו:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses a difficulty regarding smaller volumes by explaining that while the deduction is fixed per כור, smaller amounts still require a deduction based on the proportional calculation of expected loss, as they lack the 'extra' space to absorb the loss.

steps.title: Challenge: Rice loses more than the stated rate
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the Mishna's rate for rice, noting that rice actually loses a greater amount than the Mishna states.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara opens with a challenge to the Mishna's stated rate for rice.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara notes that rice is known to lose a significant amount of weight/volume, more than the nine half-kav per kor stated in the Mishna. This implies the Mishna's rate is too low for unshelled rice.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the known high loss rate of rice.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a challenge to the Mishna's rate, not a challenge to the principle of deduction.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need to specify the type of rice the Mishna is discussing.

phrases.english: GEMARA: Rice, much is lacking!

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן:
phrases.english: Rabba bar bar Ḥana says that Rabbi Yoḥanan says:
With shelled rice they taught.

phrases.aramaic: לְכוּסְּמִין וּלְזֶרַע פִּשְׁתָּן שְׁלֹשָׁה סְאִין לְכוֹר וְכוּ׳.
phrases.english: For spelt and for flaxseed, three se’a per kor, etc.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן אָמַר רַבִּי חִיָּיא:
phrases.english: Rabbi Yoḥanan says that Rabbi Ḥiyya says:
Flaxseed on its stalks they taught.
It is also taught in a baraita thus:
For spelt and for flaxseed on its stalks,
and for rice that is not shelled –
three se’a per kor.

rashi.hebrew: גמ' זרע פשתן בגבעולין - חסר כל כך לפי שהגבעולין מתייבשין ונופלים והרוח מנשבתן אבל זרע פשתן נקי אינו חסר כ"כ:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that זרע פשתן בגבעולין loses significant weight because the stalks dry out and fall off, carried away by the wind, whereas clean flaxseed does not lose as much.

tosafot.hebrew: ולאורז שאינו קלוף ג' סאין לכור - וא"ת ואמאי לא חשיב במתניתין אורז שאינו קלוף עם זרע פשתן כיון דאית להו חד שיעורא וי"ל דפשוט לו דבאורז תתחסר כל כך וברישא גבי אורז קלוף לא חשיב זרע פשתן קלוף דשמא דינם חלוק:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות asks why the Mishna does not group unshelled rice with flaxseed since they share the same loss rate, and answers that the Mishna assumes rice loses so much weight that it is obvious, while it omits shelled flaxseed in the first clause because its law might differ.

steps.title: Baraita clarifying the Mishna's scaling rule
steps.stepSummary: A baraita clarifies that the Mishna's rates apply per kor and per year.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא cites a ברייתא that clarifies the משנה's scaling rule. This is a setup line establishing the text's parameters.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ברייתא explicitly states the rates apply per כור and per year. This confirms the deductions scale with both volume and duration.

phrases.english: All according to the measure, etc. A Tanna taught:
So for each and every kor,
And so for each and every year.

steps.triggerLanguage: הַכֹּל לְפִי הַמִּדָּה וְכוּ׳. תָּנָא: כֵּן לְכׇל כּוֹר וָכוֹר, וְכֵן לְכׇל שָׁנָה וְשָׁנָה.

rashi.hebrew: לכל כור וכור - פירוש דמתניתין הוא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the baraita's phrase 'לכל כור וכור' is merely clarifying the Mishna's own wording, not introducing a new concept.

steps.title: Baraita refuting רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן בֶּן נוּרִי's view
steps.stepSummary: A baraita refutes Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri's view by stating that produce is lost and scattered, not just eaten by mice.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא cites a ברייתא refuting רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן בֶּן נוּרִי's view. This is a challenge to his dissenting opinion.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ברייתא refutes the claim that mice eat a fixed amount. It adds that produce is lost and scattered, and these losses scale with volume.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן בֶּן נוּרִי וְכוּ׳.
phrases.english: Rabbi Yoḥanan ben Nuri said, etc.
It is taught, they said to Rabbi Yoḥanan:
Many losses from them,
Many scatterings from them.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן בֶּן נוּרִי וְכוּ׳. תַּנְיָא, אָמְרוּ לוֹ לְרַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: הַרְבֵּה אוֹבְדוֹת מֵהֶן, הַרְבֵּה מִתְפַּזְּרוֹת מֵהֶן.

steps.title: Baraita specifying the conditions for deduction
steps.stepSummary: A baraita specifies that deductions apply only when the bailee mixes the deposited produce with his own.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא cites a ברייתא specifying when deductions apply. This is a setup line limiting the rule's scope.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ברייתא limits deductions to cases where the bailee mixed the produce with his own. If kept separate, no deductions apply because the owner can see the produce.

keyTerms.meaning: that he mixed them with his produce
he designated a corner for it

phrases.english: It is taught: In what matters are these words said?
That he mixed them with his fruits.
But he designated for him a corner of a corner,
He says to him: Behold yours is before you.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּנָא: בַּמֶּה דְּבָרִים אֲמוּרִים? שֶׁעֵירְבָן עִם פֵּירוֹתָיו. אֲבָל יִחֵד לוֹ קֶרֶן זָוִית, אוֹמֵר לוֹ: הֲרֵי שֶׁלְּךָ לְפָנֶיךָ.

steps.title: Challenge: Why can't the bailee calculate the loss proportionately?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the baraita: if the produce is mixed, why can't the bailee calculate the loss proportionately based on his own produce?
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא challenges the ברייתא's limitation on deductions. This is a direct challenge to the logic of the previous step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא questions why the bailee cannot calculate loss proportionately. He can observe loss in his own produce and apply that rate to the deposit.

keyTerms.meaning: let him see how much his own [produce] was
when he took supplies from them

phrases.aramaic: וְכִי עֵירְבָן עִם פֵּירוֹתָיו מַאי הָוֵי?
phrases.english: And when he mixed them with his produce, what is the law?

phrases.aramaic: לִיחְזֵי לְדִידֵיהּ כַּמָּה הָוְיָין! בְּמִסְתַּפֵּק מֵהֶם.
phrases.english: Let him see for himself how much there was! From what suffices from them.
phrases.notes: The bailee should calculate the initial amount and the remaining amount to determine the loss.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְכִי עֵירְבָן עִם פֵּירוֹתָיו מַאי הָוֵי? לִיחְזֵי לְדִידֵיהּ כַּמָּה הָוְיָין! בְמִסְתַּפֵּק מֵהֶם.

rashi.hebrew: ליחזי דידיה כמה הוו כו' - ויראה כמה חסרו ויטול על של חברו כפי חשבון:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's challenge suggests the bailee should observe the loss in his own produce and deduct a proportional amount from the deposited produce based on that calculation.

steps.title: Challenge: Why can't the bailee account for the supplies he took?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the resolution: why can't the bailee account for the supplies he took and include them in the calculation?
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא challenges the resolution by questioning accounting for supplies. This is a follow-up challenge to the previous logic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא questions why the bailee cannot account for supplies taken. This would allow accurate deduction, contradicting the need for a standard rate.

keyTerms.meaning: and let him see how much he took as supplies

phrases.english: And let him see how much he took!
Because I do not know how much he took.

steps.title: Question: How large is a large measure?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks how large a measure is considered large for Rabbi Yehuda's opinion.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא asks a neutral question about the definition of a large measure. This is a setup line for a new definition.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא asks what threshold defines a large measure according to רַבִּי יְהוּדָה. This sets up the need to define the term used in the משנה.

keyTerms.meaning: how large is a large measure

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אוֹמֵר: אִם הָיְתָה וְכוּ׳.
phrases.english: Rabbi Yehuda says: If it was a large measure, etc.
How much is a large measure?

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן:
phrases.english: Rabba bar bar Ḥana says that Rabbi Yoḥanan says:

phrases.aramaic: עֲשָׂרָה כּוֹרִין. תַּנְיָא נָמֵי הָכִי: כַּמָּה מִדָּה מְרוּבָּה?
phrases.english: Ten kor. A baraita also teaches so: How much is a large measure?
Ten kor.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אוֹמֵר: אִם הָיְתָה וְכוּ׳. כַּמָּה מִדָּה מְרוּבָּה?

tosafot.hebrew: מדה מרובה י' כורין - וא"ת אפי' ב' כורין או ג' מתנפחין לפי חשבון החסרונות העולה להם וי"ל דבי' כורין עולה להם הנפוח חסרון של כור אחד ולא יוציא להם חסרונות היינו כל החסרון דחסרון של כור אחד לא יוציא ולהכי נקט י' כורים לאפוקי פחות:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the threshold of עשרה כורים is necessary because only at that volume does the inflation (נפוח) equal the missing measure of one כור, thereby preventing the seller from claiming the missing amount is merely due to inflation.

steps.title: Tanna's statement on the conditions for deduction
steps.stepSummary: A Tanna states that deductions apply only when the produce is measured from the threshing floor and returned from the threshing floor.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא cites a תנא's statement specifying when deductions apply. This is a setup line limiting the rule's scope.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The תנא limits deductions to cases measured and returned from the threshing floor. If returned from the bailee's house, no deductions apply due to settling or moisture.

keyTerms.meaning: he measured for him from his threshing floor
he returned to him from his threshing floor

phrases.aramaic: תָּנֵי תַּנָּא קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַב נַחְמָן: בַּמֶּה דְּבָרִים אֲמוּרִים?
phrases.english: A tanna taught before Rav Naḥman: In what case is this said?

phrases.aramaic: שֶׁמָּדַד לוֹ מִתּוֹךְ גּוֹרְנוֹ וְהֶחְזִיר לוֹ מִתּוֹךְ גּוֹרְנוֹ.
phrases.english: He measured for him from his threshing floor and returned to him from his threshing floor.

phrases.aramaic: אֲבָל מָדַד לוֹ מִתּוֹךְ גּוֹרְנוֹ וְהֶחְזִיר
phrases.english: But he measured for him from his threshing floor and returned
to him from his house –

phrases.aramaic: אֵינוֹ יוֹצִיא לוֹ חֶסְרוֹנוֹת, מִפְּנֵי שֶׁמּוֹתִירוֹת.
phrases.english: he does not deduct his decrease, because of the surpluses.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּנֵי תַּנָּא קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַב נַחְמָן: בַּמֶּה דְּבָרִים אֲמוּרִים? שֶׁמָּדַד לוֹ מִתּוֹךְ גּוֹרְנוֹ וְהֶחְזִיר לוֹ מִתּוֹךְ גּוֹרְנוֹ. אֲבָל מָדַד לוֹ מִתּוֹךְ גּוֹרְנוֹ וְהֶחְזִיר לוֹ מִתּוֹךְ בֵּיתוֹ – אֵינוֹ יוֹצִיא לוֹ חֶסְרוֹנוֹת, מִפְּנֵי שֶׁמּוֹתִירוֹת.

rashi.hebrew: מדת הגורן - היתה יתירה על של בתים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the measure used at the threshing floor (גורן) is larger than the measure used in houses, which is why deductions are permitted when both measuring and returning occur at the גורן.

steps.title: רַב נַחְמָן's challenge: Are we dealing with fools?
steps.stepSummary: Rav Nachman challenges the Tanna's statement: are we dealing with fools who give with a large measure and take with a small one?
steps.whatsHappening: רַב נַחְמָן challenges the תנא's statement by questioning the assumption of different measures. This is a direct challenge to the previous step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב נַחְמָן challenges the logic that the owner used a large measure and the bailee a small one. This implies the same measure was used, contradicting the תנא's assumption.

keyTerms.term: יָהֲבִי בִּכְיָילָא רַבָּא וְשָׁקְלִי בִּכְיָילָא זוּטָא
keyTerms.meaning: give with a large measure and take with a small one

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: וְכִי בְּשׁוּפְטָנֵי עָסְקִינַן דְּיָהֲבִי בִּכְיָילָא רַבָּא
phrases.english: He said to him: And are we dealing with fools, who give with a large measure
and take with a small measure?
Perhaps you are saying about the days of the threshing floor.
In what matter are these words said –

phrases.aramaic: שֶׁמָּדַד לוֹ בִּימוֹת הַגּוֹרֶן וְהֶחְזִיר לוֹ בִּימוֹת הַגּוֹרֶן.
phrases.english: He measured for him in the days of the threshing floor and returned to him in the days of the…

phrases.aramaic: אֲבָל מָדַד לוֹ בִּימוֹת הַגּוֹרֶן וְהֶחְזִיר
phrases.english: But he measured for him in the days of the threshing floor and returned
to him in the days of the rains –

phrases.aramaic: אֵינוֹ יוֹצִיא לוֹ חֶסְרוֹן מִפְּנֵי שֶׁמּוֹתִירוֹת.
phrases.english: he does not deduct for him the loss because of the remainders.
phrases.notes: Refers to the produce absorbing moisture and expanding during the rainy season.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: וְכִי בְשׁוּפְטָנֵי עָסְקִינַן דְיָהֲבִי בִכְיָילָא רַבָּא וְשָקְלִי בִכְיָילָא זוּטָא?

rashi.hebrew: בשופטני - שוטים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the term שופטני refers to fools, highlighting Rav Nachman's rhetorical question about whether the scenario involves irrational behavior.

rashi.hebrew: בכיילא - במדה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that כיילא means a measure, specifying the object of discussion in the dispute.

rashi.hebrew: בימות הגורן - סמוך ליובשן שנתיבשו בחמה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines ימות הגורן as the time when produce is dry and has shrunk due to sun exposure, explaining the context of the larger measure.

rashi.hebrew: והחזיר לו בימות הגשמים - חזרו ונתפחו מלחלוחית הגשם והקור:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that during ימות הגשמים, the produce swells due to moisture and cold, accounting for the smaller measure when returned.

steps.title: Challenge: The jug should burst
steps.stepSummary: Rav Pappa challenges the resolution: if the produce expands during the rainy season, the jug should burst.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a physical objection to the previous resolution. Rav Pappa argues that if produce truly expands, the container holding it would rupture.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This objection targets the assumption that expansion occurs without physical consequence. If the volume increases significantly, the rigid container cannot accommodate it, implying the expansion theory is flawed.

phrases.english: Rav Pappa said to Abaye:
If so, let the jug burst!
There was an incident and the jug burst.
If you wish, say, on account of compression.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב פָּפָּא לְאַבָּיֵי: אִם כֵן, לִפְקַע כַדָּא!

rashi.hebrew: א"כ - שדרכו לתפוח:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase אִם כֵּן implies that the produce is of a type that normally expands.

rashi.hebrew: לפקע כדא - כשממלאין כד חיטין או שעורין בימות הגורן וגפין את הכד יפקע בימות הגשמים מחמת תפיחת התבואה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that לפקע כדא means the jug would burst during the rainy season due to the expansion of the grain stored in it during the harvest season.

rashi.hebrew: איצצא - דוחק מתוך שהם במקום צר נדחקו יחד ואין תפוחין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that איצצא refers to the produce being compressed in a tight space, preventing it from expanding.

steps.title: Mishna: Deductions for wine and oil
steps.stepSummary: The Mishna establishes the standard deductions for wine (one-sixth or one-fifth) and oil (three log per hundred, split between sediment and absorption), and notes exceptions for refined oil and old casks.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya introduces the Mishnaic baseline for commercial deductions on liquids. It establishes standard rates for wine and oil, then lists specific exceptions.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishna sets a one-sixth deduction for wine and a three-log rate for oil, split between sediment and absorption. It then exempts refined oil from sediment deductions and old casks from absorption deductions, with Rabbi Yehuda adding a final ruling on refined oil.

phrases.english: MISHNA: He deducts for him one-sixth for wine.
Rabbi Yehuda says: One-fifth.

phrases.aramaic: יוֹצִיא לוֹ שְׁלֹשָׁה לוּגִּין שֶׁמֶן לְמֵאָה:
phrases.english: He deducts for him three log of oil for one hundred:
A log and a half for sediment,
A log and a half for absorption.
If it was refined oil –
He does not deduct for him sediment.
If they were old casks –
He does not deduct for him absorption.
Rabbi Yehuda says:

phrases.aramaic: אַף הַמּוֹכֵר שֶׁמֶן מְזוּקָּק לַחֲבֵירוֹ כׇּל
phrases.english: Even the one who sells refined oil to his fellow all
The days of the year –

phrases.aramaic: הֲרֵי זֶה מְקַבֵּל עָלָיו לוֹג וּמֶחֱצָה שְׁמָרִים לְמֵאָה.
phrases.english: This one accepts upon himself a log and a half of sediment for one hundred.

rashi.hebrew: מתני' ליין - אם הפקיד אצלו יין ועירבו עם יינו הקנקנים בולעין שתות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishna's deduction of one-sixth for wine applies when someone deposits wine with another and the casks absorb it.

rashi.hebrew: אף המוכר כו' - כשם שאמרו במפקיד כן אמרו במוכר שהמוכר שמן לחבירו ונותן לו מתוך חביותיו כשהוא מסתפק תמיד והוא מזוקק הרי הלוקח מקבל עליו לפחות לו לוג ומחצה מחמת שמרים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that Rabbi Yehuda's ruling about refined oil sold year-round applies to a seller who continuously supplies oil from his casks, where the buyer accepts a deduction for sediment.

steps.title: Resolution: Local customs and cask materials
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the dispute between the first Tanna and Rabbi Yehuda by attributing it to differing local customs regarding cask coatings and soil quality.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the apparent contradiction between the first Tanna and Rabbi Yehuda. It attributes the difference to varying local materials rather than a halachic dispute.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution posits that different regions use different cask coatings or soil qualities. Wax coatings absorb less, while pitch absorbs more, explaining the differing deduction rates without contradicting the underlying principle.

keyTerms.meaning: quality of the earth/soil for casks

phrases.english: And they do not disagree:

phrases.aramaic: מָר כִּי אַתְרֵיהּ וּמָר כִּי אַתְרֵיהּ.
phrases.english: This Sage according to his place, and this Sage according to his place.

phrases.aramaic: בְּאַתְרֵיהּ דְּמָר חָפוּ בְּקִירָא וְלָא מָיֵיץ טְפֵי,
phrases.english: In the place of this Sage, they coat with wax and it does not absorb much,

phrases.aramaic: בְּאַתְרֵיהּ דְּמָר חָפוּ בְּכוּפְרָא וּמָיֵיץ טְפֵי.
phrases.english: In the place of this Sage, they coat with pitch and it absorbs much.
If you wish, say instead due to the quality of earth,

phrases.aramaic: הָא מָיְיצָא טְפֵי וְהָא לָא מָיְיצָא טְפֵי.
phrases.english: This one absorbs much and this one does not absorb much.

rashi.hebrew: גמ' חפו בקירא - טחו החביות מבפנים בשעוה כדרך שעושין אנו בזפת:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that חָפוּ בְקִירָא means the casks were coated internally with wax, similar to how we use pitch today.

rashi.hebrew: כופרא - זפת:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י identifies כוּפְרָא as pitch.

rashi.hebrew: גרגישתא - קרקע שעושין ממנה החביות ארדלי"א בלע"ז:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines גַּרְגִּישְׁתָּא as the earth/clay from which the casks are made.

rashi.hebrew: הא - באתריה דר"י מייץ טפי מההיא דאתריה דתנא קמא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that הָא מָיְיצָא טְפֵי refers to רבי יהודה's location having greater absorption than the תנא קמא's location.

steps.title: Rav Yehuda's calculation of wine deductions
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara presents a detailed calculation from Rav Yehuda's locale showing how the one-sixth deduction is applied in practice.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא illustrates the deduction rules with a specific numerical example from Rav Yehuda's locale. It breaks down the costs, sales, and deductions for a standard barrel.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rav Yehuda sells 36 of 48 pitchers to cover the barrel cost. From the remaining 12, he deducts 8 pitchers for absorption (one-sixth of the total). The remaining 4 pitchers constitute his profit.

phrases.aramaic: בְּאַתְרֵיהּ דְּרַב יְהוּדָה רָמוּ אַרְבָּעִים וְתַמְנֵי כּוּזֵי בְּדַנָּא.
phrases.english: In Rav Yehuda's place, they put forty-eight pitchers in a barrel.
The barrel goes for six dinars,

phrases.aramaic: פָּרֵיס רַב יְהוּדָה שִׁיתָּא שִׁיתָּא בְּזוּזָא,
phrases.english: Rav Yehuda divided six six for a dinar,
for thirty-six for six,
twelve remained for him.

phrases.aramaic: דַּל תְּמָנְיָא שְׁתוּתֵי, פָּשׁוּ לְהוּ אַרְבְּעָה.
phrases.english: for eight sixths, four remained for them.

steps.triggerLanguage: בְּאַתְרֵיהּ דְּרַב יְהוּדָה רָמוּ אַרְבָּעִים וְתַמְנֵי כּוּזֵי...

rashi.hebrew: באתריה דרב יהודה - מילתא באפי נפשיה היא ולאו אמתניתין קאי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that this calculation is an independent statement (מילתא באפי נפשיה) and does not directly address the Mishnah's ruling.

rashi.hebrew: רמו ארבעין ותמניא כוזי בדנא - מדה קטנה היתה במקומו שארבעים ושמונה ממלאין את החבית:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the local measure was small, such that 48 pitchers filled a single barrel.

rashi.hebrew: אזיל דנא בשיתא - בששה זוזים היתה נמכרת ביחד בימות הבציר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the barrel was sold for six dinars during the harvest season.

rashi.hebrew: פריס ליה רב יהודה שיתא שיתא בזוזא - נעשה רב יהודה חנווני והיה מוכרן שש כוזי בזוז:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י describes Rav Yehuda acting as a merchant who sold the wine at a rate of six pitchers per dinar.
